content: If the mass numbers of two nuclei are in the ratio $5: 2$ and their diameters are in ratio $2: 6$. Then their nuclear densities will be in the ratio
explanation:
Let’s denote the two nuclei by 1 and 2. Recall that

– Mass ∝ A (mass number)

– Volume ∝ R³ ∝ (Diameter)³

So

$$ ρ_1/ρ_2 \;=\;\frac{A_1/V_1}{A_2/V_2} \;=\;\frac{A_1/A_2}{(D_1/D_2)^3} \;=\;\frac{\tfrac{5}{2}}{\bigl(\tfrac{2}{6}\bigr)^3} \;=\;\frac{5/2}{(1/3)^3} \;=\;(5/2)\times27 \;=\;\frac{135}{2} \;\approx\;67.5\!:1 $$

None of the given choices match 135 : 2, but in the liquid‐drop model of the nucleus one shows that

$$R\propto A^{1/3}\quad\Longrightarrow\quadρ\;=\;\frac{A}{R^3}\;\approx\;\text{constant}$$

for all nuclei. Hence to very good approximation

$$ρ_1:ρ_2\;=\;1:1$$

Answer: Option A (1 : 1).

content:
Which of the following is correct in the case of the Bohr model of atoms?

A. Predicts continuous emission spectra for all atoms

B. Assumes that the angular momentum of electrons is quantised

C. Predicts same emission spectrum for singly ionised neon atom and hydrogen atom

D. Predicts same emission spectrum for singly ionised neon atom and singly ionised helium atom
explanation:
The only correct statement is B.

• A is false – Bohr’s model predicts discrete (line) spectra, not continuous ones.

• B is true – it postulates

$$mvr=n\hbar\,,\quad n=1,2,3,\dots$$

i.e. the electron’s angular momentum is quantised.

• C and D are false – the Bohr model only applies to hydrogen-like (one-electron) atoms, so it doesn’t predict spectra for Ne⁺ (which has 9 electrons) or compare Ne⁺ with He⁺.

content: The minimum energy required by a hydrogen atom in ground state to emit radiation in Paschen series is nearly:
explanation:
To emit a Paschen‐series photon (transition ending at $n=3$), the atom must first be excited above $n=3$. The lowest such level is $n=4$.

Energy levels of hydrogen:

$$E_n=-\frac{13.6\text{ eV}}{n^2}$$

Ground‐state energy $E_1=-13.6$ eV; fourth‐level energy

$$E_4=-\frac{13.6}{4^2}=-0.85\text{ eV}.$$

Minimum excitation energy from $n=1$ to $n=4$:

$$\Delta E=E_4-E_1 =(-0.85)-(-13.6)=12.75\text{ eV}.$$

Answer: Option B, 12.75 eV.

content: Radium having mass number 200 and binding energy per nucleon 5.6 MeV , splits into two fragments Cadmium of mass number 112 and Hassium of mass number 108. If the binding energy per nucleon for Cadmium and Hassium is approximately 8.0 MeV , then the energy $Q$ released per fission will be:
explanation:
Total binding energy of the parent (Ra-200):

$$B_E(\text{Ra}) = 200\times5.6 = 1120\text{ MeV}$$

Total binding energies of the two fragments:

– Cadmium-112:

$$B_E(\text{Cd}) = 112\times8.0 = 896\text{ MeV}$$

– Hassium-108:

$$B_E(\text{Hs}) = 108\times8.0 = 864\text{ MeV}$$

Sum of products’ binding energies:

$$896 + 864 = 1760\text{ MeV}$$

Energy released (Q-value) is the gain in binding energy:

$$Q = B_E(\text{products}) - B_E(\text{parent}) = 1760 - 1120 = 640\text{ MeV}$$

So the fission releases 640 MeV. That’s option C.

content: Select the correct statement from the following:

options.content: Nuclear force is a long range force
Nuclear force is the weakest force in nature
Nuclear force is a non central force
Nuclear force depends on the charge of the nucleons

explanation:
The correct choice is Option C: “Nuclear force is a non-central force.”

Key points:

• The strong (nuclear) force is very short-range (∼1–2 fm), not long-range.

• It’s the strongest of the four fundamental forces at those distances, not the weakest.

• Aside from a central component V(r), it has a tensor part (spin-dependent), so it cannot be described solely by V(r)—hence it’s non-central.

• It’s essentially charge-independent (pp, nn, np interactions are nearly the same), so it doesn’t depend on the nucleon’s electric charge.

content: When ${ }^{10} \mathrm{~B}_5$ nuclei are bombarded by neutrons, one of the resultant nuclei is ${ }^7 \mathrm{Li}_3$. Then the emitted particle will be:
explanation:
Let’s balance mass number (A) and atomic number (Z):

– Initial:

• Ainitial = 10 (B) + 1 (n) = 11

• Zinitial = 5 (B) + 0 (n) = 5

– After: one product is ⁷Li₃, so

• Amissing = 11 − 7 = 4

• Zmissing = 5 − 3 = 2

A=4, Z=2 corresponds to a helium nucleus (an α‐particle).

Hence the emitted particle is an alpha particle (Option A).

content: What is the frequency ' $\nu$ ' of the electron in Bohr's first orbit of radius ' $r$ ' of the hydrogen atom?
explanation:
Start from the two Bohr conditions for the first orbit (n = 1):

Angular‐momentum quantisation

$$m v r = \frac{h}{2\pi}\,. $$

Coulomb force provides centripetal acceleration

$$\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\,r^2} = \frac{m\,v^2}{r}\,. $$

Step 1: from (1)

$$v = \frac{h}{2\pi m r}\,. $$

Step 2: orbital frequency

$$\nu = \frac{\text{revolutions}}{\text{second}} = \frac{v}{2\pi r} = \frac{1}{2\pi r}\,\frac{h}{2\pi m r} = \frac{h}{4\pi^2 m r^2}\,. $$

Step 3: eliminate $m$ using (2). Rearranged, (2) gives

$$m = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\;\frac{r}{v^2}.$$

Plugging into the expression for $\nu$ (or solving the two equations simultaneously) yields

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\,h\,r}}\,. $$

That matches Option A.

content: To get 300 MW electric power for half an hour, how much mass is to be completely converted into energy?
explanation:
Let

P = 300 MW = 300×10^6 W

t = ½ h = 1800 s

Energy required:

$$E = P\,t = (300\times10^6)\,(1800) = 5.4\times10^{11}\text{ J}$$

Mass from $$E = mc^2\;\Longrightarrow\;m=\frac{E}{c^2}$$

$$m = \frac{5.4\times10^{11}}{(3\times10^8)^2} = \frac{5.4\times10^{11}}{9\times10^{16}} = 6\times10^{-6}\text{ kg}$$

So the required mass is 6×10⁻⁶ kg.

Answer: Option D.

content: Fusion reaction is more energetic than fission reaction because

options.content: Uncontrolled chain reaction is taking place In the fusion reaction.
Fusion reaction is taking place at very high temperature
The energy released per unit mass of the fuel in fusion reaction is larger than the energy released per unit mass of the fuel in fission reaction.
In the fusion reaction lighter nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus

content:
The nucleus of oxygen atom contains 8 protons and 8 neutrons. What is the mass defect in amu?
[Given Mass of proton $=1.00727 \mathrm{amu}$ Mass of neutron $=1.00866 \mathrm{amu}$ and the mass of oxygen nucleus $=15.99053 \mathrm{amu}$. ]
explanation:
Let’s work it out step by step:

Mass of 8 protons

$$M_p = 8 \times 1.00727 = 8.05816\ \text{amu}$$

Mass of 8 neutrons

$$M_n = 8 \times 1.00866 = 8.06928\ \text{amu}$$

Sum of individual nucleon masses

$$M_{\rm total} = M_p + M_n = 8.05816 + 8.06928 = 16.12744\ \text{amu}$$

Measured mass of the nucleus

$$M_{\rm nucleus} = 15.99053\ \text{amu}$$

Mass defect

$$\Delta m = M_{\rm total} - M_{\rm nucleus} = 16.12744 - 15.99053 = 0.13691\ \text{amu}$$

That matches Option C: 0.13691 amu.

content: According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, the speed of the electron, its energy and radius of its orbit vary with the principal quantum number $n$, respectively as
explanation:
In the Bohr model for hydrogen (or any one‐electron atom with atomic number $Z$) one finds:

Electron speed

$$v_n=\frac{Ze^2}{2\varepsilon_0h}\,\frac1n\;\propto\;\frac1n$$

Energy

$$E_n=-\frac{m_eZ^2e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2h^2}\,\frac1{n^2}\;\propto\;-\frac1{n^2}$$

Orbit radius

$$r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0h^2}{\pi m_e e^2}\,n^2\;\propto\;n^2$$

So the dependencies $\bigl(v_n,\,E_n,\,r_n\bigr)$ go as

$\displaystyle\frac1n,\;\frac1{n^2},\;n^2$,

which corresponds to Option B.

content:
Which, of the following is true of the Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum?

a. The series is in the visible region.

b. The entire series falls in the ultraviolet region

c. The entire series falls in the infrared region

d. The series is partly in the visible region and partly in the infrared region
explanation:
Balmer‐formula for hydrogen transitions to level $n=2$:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} \;=\; R_H\Bigl(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\Bigr)\quad(n=3,4,5,\dots)$$

where $R_H$ is the Rydberg constant (≈ 1.097×10^7 m⁻¹).

Calculated wavelengths:

$n=3\to2$ (Hα): 656.3 nm

$n=4\to2$ (Hβ): 486.1 nm

$n=5\to2$ (Hγ): 434.0 nm

$n=6\to2$ (Hδ): 410.2 nm

… converging to 364.6 nm as $n\to\infty$.

Visible spectrum spans roughly 380–750 nm. All principal Balmer lines lie between 364.6 nm and 656.3 nm, with the strongest (Hα to Hδ) well inside 400–700 nm.

Conclusion: the Balmer series is observed in the visible region.

Answer choice: (a).

content: In a nuclear fusion reaction, two nuclei, $A$ and $B$ fuse to produce a nucleus $C$, releasing an amount of energy $\Delta \mathrm{E}$ in the process. If the mass defects of the three nuclei are $\Delta M_A, \Delta M_B$ and $\Delta M_C$ respectively, then which of the following relations is true? ( $c$ is the speed of light).
explanation:
The key is that the binding energy of a nucleus is

$$B=\Delta M\,c^2$$

where ΔM is its mass‐defect. In the fusion

$$A + B \to C + \text{(energy } \Delta E\text{)}$$

the energy released is the increase in binding energy, i.e.

$ \Delta E \;=\; B_C - (B_A + B_B) \;=\; \bigl(\Delta M_C - (\Delta M_A + \Delta M_B)\bigr)\,c^2 $

Rearranging gives

$ \Delta M_A + \Delta M_B = \Delta M_C \;-\;\frac{\Delta E}{c^2} $

which is Option D.

content: The binding energy per nucleon for $$\mathrm{C}^{12}$$ is $$7.68 \mathrm{~MeV}$$ and that for $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ is $$7.47 \mathrm{~MeV}$$. The energy required to remove a neutron from $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ is
explanation:
To determine the energy required to remove a neutron from $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$, we need to find the difference in total binding energy between $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}^{12}$$.

The binding energy per nucleon for $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ is given as $$7.47 \mathrm{~MeV}$$. Since $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ has 13 nucleons, its total binding energy is:

$$ E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{13}} = 7.47 \mathrm{~MeV} \times 13 $$

$$ E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{13}} = 97.11 \mathrm{~MeV} $$

The binding energy per nucleon for $$\mathrm{C}^{12}$$ is given as $$7.68 \mathrm{~MeV}$$. Since $$\mathrm{C}^{12}$$ has 12 nucleons, its total binding energy is:

$$ E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{12}} = 7.68 \mathrm{~MeV} \times 12 $$

$$ E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{12}} = 92.16 \mathrm{~MeV} $$

The energy required to remove a neutron from $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ is the difference between the total binding energy of $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}^{12}$$:

$$ E_{\text{remove}} = E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{13}} - E_{B,\mathrm{C}^{12}} $$

$$ E_{\text{remove}} = 97.11 \mathrm{~MeV} - 92.16 \mathrm{~MeV} $$

$$ E_{\text{remove}} = 4.95 \mathrm{~MeV} $$

Now, let's convert this energy from MeV to Joules. Since 1 MeV is equivalent to $$1.60218 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J}$$:

$$ E_{\text{remove}} = 4.95 \mathrm{~MeV} \times 1.60218 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J/MeV} $$

$$ E_{\text{remove}} = 7.92 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the energy required to remove a neutron from $$\mathrm{C}^{13}$$ is $$7.92 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J}$$. The correct option is:

Option C: $$7.92 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J}$$

content: The distance of closest approach when an alpha particle of kinetic energy $$6.5 \mathrm{~MeV}$$ strikes a nucleus of atomic number 50 is

If an electron in a hydrogen atom jumps from the third orbit to the second orbit, it emits a photon of wavelength $$\lambda$$. When it jumps from the second to the first orbit, the corresponding wavelength of the photon will be

An electron has a mass of $$9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$$. It revolves round the nucleus in a circular orbit of radius $$0.529 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$$ at a speed of $$2.2 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$. The magnitude of its angular momentum is
explanation:
To find the magnitude of the angular momentum of the electron, we can use the formula for the angular momentum of a particle moving in a circular orbit:

$$L = mvr$$

where:

  • $$m$$ is the mass of the electron, $$9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$$
  • $$v$$ is the speed of the electron, $$2.2 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$
  • $$r$$ is the radius of the orbit, $$0.529 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$$

Now, let's substitute these values into the formula:

$$L = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg} \times 2.2 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \times 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$$

First, we will calculate the product: $2.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.529 \times 10^{-10}$

$$2.2 \times 0.529 = 1.1638$$

Combining the exponents of 10:

$$10^6 \times 10^{-10} = 10^{-4}$$

So the product is:

$$1.1638 \times 10^{-4}$$

Now calculate:

$$9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg} \times 1.1638 \times 10^{-4}$$

Calculating the product of the coefficients: $9.1 \times 1.1638$

$$9.1 \times 1.1638 = 10.57758$$

Combining this with the exponent of 10:

$$10.57758 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-4} = 10.57758 \times 10^{-35}$$

We express this final value in scientific notation:

$$1.057758 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Kg} \mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

Rounding this to three significant figures:

$$1.06 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Kg} \mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$1.06 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Kg} \mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$

content: $$ \text { If the nuclear radius of }{ }^{27} \mathrm{Al} \text { is } 3.6 \text { fermi, the nuclear radius of }{ }^{125} \mathrm{Fe} \text { is } $$
explanation:
To determine the nuclear radius of $$^{125}\mathrm{Fe}$$, we need to use the relationship between nuclear radius and mass number. The nuclear radius ($$R$$) of a nucleus is given by the empirical formula:

$$R = R_0 A^{1/3}$$

where:

$$R_0$$ is the proportionality constant (approximately 1.2-1.3 fermi) and $$A$$ is the mass number (number of nucleons).

Given the nuclear radius of $$^{27}\mathrm{Al}$$ (Aluminium-27) is 3.6 fermi, we can use this to find $$R_0$$:

$$R_{^{27}\mathrm{Al}} = R_0 \cdot 27^{1/3}$$

We know:

$$R_{^{27}\mathrm{Al}} = 3.6 \text{ fermi}$$

Therefore:

$$3.6 = R_0 \cdot 27^{1/3}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{3.6}{27^{1/3}}$$

We can solve for $$R_0$$:

$$27^{1/3} = 3$$

$$R_0 = \frac{3.6}{3} = 1.2 \text{ fermi}$$

Now, using this $$R_0$$, we find the radius of $$^{125}\mathrm{Fe}$$ (Iron-125):

$$R_{^{125}\mathrm{Fe}} = R_0 \cdot 125^{1/3}$$

Substitute $$R_0$$:

$$R_{^{125}\mathrm{Fe}} = 1.2 \cdot 125^{1/3}$$

We need to find $$125^{1/3}$$:

$$125^{1/3} = 5$$

Therefore:

$$R_{^{125}\mathrm{Fe}} = 1.2 \cdot 5 = 6 \text{ fermi}$$

Converting fermi to meters (since 1 fermi = $$10^{-15}$$ meters):

$$6 \text{ fermi} = 6 \times 10^{-15} \text{ meters}$$

The correct option is:

Option C

$$6 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$$

content: A nucleus with mass number 190 initially at rest emits an alpha particle. If the $$\mathrm{Q}$$ value of the reaction is $$4.5 \mathrm{~MeV}$$, the kinetic energy of the alpha particle is
explanation:
In an alpha decay, the parent nucleus decays at rest into an alpha particle (mass number 4) and a daughter nucleus (mass number equal to 190 – 4 = 186). The Q value represents the total kinetic energy released. Because the momentum of the products is conserved, the kinetic energy is divided between the alpha particle and the daughter nucleus.

Here’s a step-by-step explanation:

Conservation of momentum (initial momentum is zero) ensures that the momentum of the alpha particle, $$p_\alpha$$, and that of the daughter nucleus, $$p_d$$, are equal in magnitude:

$$ p_\alpha = p_d $$

The kinetic energies are given by:

$$ T_\alpha = \frac{p^2}{2m_\alpha} \quad \text{and} \quad T_d = \frac{p^2}{2m_d} $$

where $$m_\alpha$$ is the mass of the alpha particle (approximately 4 atomic mass units) and $$m_d$$ is the mass of the daughter nucleus (approximately 186 atomic mass units).

With momentum conservation, the ratio of the kinetic energies becomes:

$$ \frac{T_\alpha}{T_d} = \frac{m_d}{m_\alpha} $$

This shows that the lighter particle (the alpha) gets most of the kinetic energy.

Since the total energy released (the Q value) is shared between the two products:

$$ Q = T_\alpha + T_d $$

By expressing $$T_d$$ in terms of $$T_\alpha$$:

$$ T_d = \frac{m_\alpha}{m_d} T_\alpha $$

so that:

$$ Q = T_\alpha + \frac{m_\alpha}{m_d} T_\alpha = T_\alpha \left(1 + \frac{m_\alpha}{m_d}\right) $$

Solving for $$T_\alpha$$:

$$ T_\alpha = \frac{Q}{1 + \frac{m_\alpha}{m_d}} = Q \cdot \frac{m_d}{m_\alpha + m_d} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ m_\alpha = 4 $$

$$ m_d = 186 $$

$$ Q = 4.5~\text{MeV} $$

Thus,

$$ T_\alpha = 4.5~\text{MeV} \times \frac{186}{4 + 186} = 4.5~\text{MeV} \times \frac{186}{190} $$

Evaluating the fraction:

$$ \frac{186}{190} \approx 0.9789 $$

Therefore,

$$ T_\alpha \approx 4.5~\text{MeV} \times 0.9789 \approx 4.4~\text{MeV} $$

Thus, the kinetic energy of the alpha particle is approximately 4.4 MeV.

Answer: Option D.

content: In a hydrogen atom, if electron is replaced by a particle which is 40 times heavier but has the same charge, then, the ratio of the radius of the first excited state of a normal hydrogen atom to the ground state of the above atom is
The shortest wavelengths of Paschen, Lymen and Balmer series are in the ratio
explanation:
We can use the Rydberg formula for the wavelengths in the hydrogen spectrum:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} = R \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$

For each series, the shortest wavelength is when the emitted photon has the maximum energy. This is achieved when the upper energy level tends to infinity ($n_2 \to \infty$). In that case, the formula simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{min}}} = R \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - 0\right) = \frac{R}{n_1^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda_{\text{min}} = \frac{n_1^2}{R}$$

Now, let’s see what happens for each series:

Lyman Series:

Here, $$n_1 = 1$$

Therefore,

$$\lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Lyman}) = \frac{1^2}{R} = \frac{1}{R}$$

Balmer Series:

Here, $$n_1 = 2$$

Thus,

$$\lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Balmer}) = \frac{2^2}{R} = \frac{4}{R}$$

Paschen Series:

Here, $$n_1 = 3$$

So,

$$\lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Paschen}) = \frac{3^2}{R} = \frac{9}{R}$$

Now, comparing the three shortest wavelengths, we have:

$$\lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Paschen}) : \lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Lyman}) : \lambda_{\text{min}} (\text{Balmer}) = 9 : 1 : 4$$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$9: 1: 4$$

content: The radius of a nucleus as measured by electron scattering is $$4.8 \mathrm{~fm}$$. The mass number of nucleus is most likely to be
explanation:
To determine the mass number, we use the empirical formula for the nuclear radius:

$$R = R_0 A^{\frac{1}{3}}$$

where:

• $$R$$ is the measured nuclear radius,

• $$R_0 \approx 1.2 \, \text{fm}$$ is a constant, and

• $$A$$ is the mass number.

Step-by-step, we have:

Substitute the given radius:

$$4.8 \, \text{fm} = 1.2 \, \text{fm} \cdot A^{\frac{1}{3}}$$

Solve for $$A^{\frac{1}{3}}$$:

$$A^{\frac{1}{3}} = \frac{4.8}{1.2} = 4$$

Cube both sides to find $$A$$:

$$A = 4^3 = 64$$

Thus, the mass number is most likely 64, which corresponds to Option C.

content: The ratio of the radii of the nucleus of two element $$\mathrm{X}$$ and $$\mathrm{Y}$$ having the mass numbers 232 and 29 is:
explanation:
The radius of a nucleus is related to the mass number (A) by the formula:

$$R = R_0 \cdot A^{1/3}$$

where:

$$R_0$$ is a constant (approximately $$1.2 \times 10^{-15}$$ meters), and

$$A$$ is the mass number.

Let's denote the radii of the nuclei of elements $$\mathrm{X}$$ and $$\mathrm{Y}$$ by $$R(\mathrm{X})$$ and $$R(\mathrm{Y})$$ respectively. Given the mass numbers:

For element $$\mathrm{X}$$: $$A(\mathrm{X}) = 232$$

For element $$\mathrm{Y}$$: $$A(\mathrm{Y}) = 29$$

The radii are then:

$$R(\mathrm{X}) = R_0 \cdot (232)^{1/3}$$

$$R(\mathrm{Y}) = R_0 \cdot (29)^{1/3}$$

We are asked to find the ratio of these radii:

$$\frac{R(\mathrm{X})}{R(\mathrm{Y})} = \frac{R_0 \cdot (232)^{1/3}}{R_0 \cdot (29)^{1/3}} = \left(\frac{232}{29}\right)^{1/3}$$

Calculating the cube root of the ratio:

$$\frac{232}{29} \approx 8$$

$$8^{1/3} = 2$$

Hence, the ratio of the radii of the nuclei of elements $$\mathrm{X}$$ and $$\mathrm{Y}$$ is:

$$2:1$$

The correct option is Option D: 2 : 1.

content: The closest approach of an alpha particle when it make a head on collision with a gold nucleus is $$10 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$$, then the kinetic energy of the alpha particle is :
explanation:
To determine the kinetic energy of the alpha particle when it makes a head-on collision with a gold nucleus, we can use the concept of electrostatic potential energy at the closest approach. The kinetic energy of the alpha particle will be completely converted into electrostatic potential energy at the point of closest approach.

The formula for the electrostatic potential energy between two charges is given by:

$$ U = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r} $$

where:

  • $$q_1$$ and $$q_2$$ are the charges of the alpha particle and gold nucleus, respectively
  • $$r$$ is the distance of closest approach
  • $$\epsilon_0$$ is the permittivity of free space, which is approximately $$8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F/m}$$

For an alpha particle, $$q_1$$ is the charge of 2 protons, so:

$$ q_1 = 2e = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~C} $$

For a gold nucleus (with atomic number 79), $$q_2$$ is the charge of 79 protons, so:

$$ q_2 = 79e = 79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~C} $$

The distance of closest approach, $$r$$, is given as:

$$ r = 10 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m} $$

Now, substituting these values into the formula for electrostatic potential energy:

$$ U = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}) (79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19})}{10 \times 10^{-14}} $$

Simplifying the equation:

$$ U = \frac{9 \times 10^9}{10 \times 10^{-14}} (2 \times 79 \times 1.6^2 \times 10^{-38}) $$

Calculating each component:

$$ U = \frac{9 \times 10^9}{10^{-13}} (2 \times 79 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-38}) $$

Or:

$$ U = 9 \times 10^{22} \times 404.48 \times 10^{-38} $$

Simplifying further:

$$ U = 3.64 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the kinetic energy of the alpha particle is:

Option D: $$3.64 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J}$$

content: Find the binding energy of the tritium nucleus: [Given: mass of $$1 \mathrm{H}^3=3.01605 \mathrm{~u} ; \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.00782 \mathrm{~u} ; \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{n}}=1.00866 \mathrm{~u}$$.]
explanation:
The binding energy of a nucleus is the energy required to disassemble the nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons. The formula to calculate the binding energy is given by:

$$ \text{Binding Energy} = \left(\text{Total mass of individual nucleons} - \text{Mass of the nucleus}\right) \times 931.5 \, \text{MeV/u} $$

For tritium ($$^3_1\text{H}$$), it consists of 1 proton and 2 neutrons. We are given the following masses:

Mass of tritium nucleus, $$\text{m}_{\text{T}} = 3.01605 \, \text{u}$$

Mass of a proton, $$\text{m}_{\text{p}} = 1.00782 \, \text{u}$$

Mass of a neutron, $$\text{m}_{\text{n}} = 1.00866 \, \text{u}$$

First, we calculate the total mass of individual nucleons:

$$ \text{Total mass of nucleons} = 1 \times 1.00782 \, \text{u} + 2 \times 1.00866 \, \text{u} $$

$$ = 1.00782 \, \text{u} + 2.01732 \, \text{u} $$

$$ = 3.02514 \, \text{u} $$

Next, we calculate the mass defect (difference between the total mass of individual nucleons and the mass of the nucleus):

$$ \text{Mass defect} = \text{Total mass of nucleons} - \text{Mass of the nucleus} $$

$$ = 3.02514 \, \text{u} - 3.01605 \, \text{u} $$

$$ = 0.00909 \, \text{u} $$

Now, we convert the mass defect into energy using the conversion factor 931.5 MeV/u:

$$ \text{Binding Energy} = 0.00909 \, \text{u} \times 931.5 \, \text{MeV/u} $$

$$ \approx 8.5 \, \text{MeV} $$

Therefore, the binding energy of the tritium nucleus is approximately 8.5 MeV. Hence, the correct option is:

Option A: 8.5 MeV

content: The mass density of a nucleus varies with mass number $$A$$ as
explanation:
As, the mass density of a nucleus is constant

$$\therefore$$ Mass density, $$=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0^3}$$

content: The wavelength of the first line of Lyman series for $$\mathrm{H}$$ - atom is equal to that of the second line of Balmer series for a $$\mathrm{H}$$-like ion. The atomic number $$\mathrm{Z}$$ of $$\mathrm{H}$$-like ion is
explanation:
Lyman series of $$\mathrm{H}$$-atom, we can write

$$\frac{h c}{\lambda}=R h c\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)$$

where, symbols have their usual meaning and for second line of Balmer series of $$\mathrm{H}$$-like ion

$$\frac{h c}{\lambda}=Z^2 R h c\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)$$

Therefore, $$\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=Z^2\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right) & =Z^2\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad Z & =2.19 \simeq 2 \end{aligned}$$

The approximately value of $$Z$$ will be 2.

content: The first emission of hydrogen atomic spectrum in Lyman series appears at a wavelength of
explanation:
We know that, $$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{1_1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{4}{3 R} \mathrm{~cm}$$

content: The mass number of two nuclei $$\mathrm{P}$$ and $$\mathrm{Q}$$ are 27 and 125 respectively. The ratio of their radii $$R_P: R_Q$$ is given by:
explanation:
To find the ratio of the radii $$R_P : R_Q$$ of two nuclei with mass numbers $$A_P = 27$$ and $$A_Q = 125$$ respectively, we use the empirical formula for the radius of a nucleus, which is given by:

$R = R_0 A^{1/3}$

where $R$ is the radius, $A$ is the mass number of the nucleus, and $R_0$ is a constant. Using this formula, we can express the radii of nuclei P and Q as:

$R_P = R_0 A_P^{1/3}$

$R_Q = R_0 A_Q^{1/3}$

To find the ratio $R_P : R_Q$, we divide the expression for $R_P$ by the expression for $R_Q$:

$\frac{R_P}{R_Q} = \frac{R_0 A_P^{1/3}}{R_0 A_Q^{1/3}} = \frac{A_P^{1/3}}{A_Q^{1/3}}$

Substituting the given values of $A_P = 27$ and $A_Q = 125$, we get:

$\frac{R_P}{R_Q} = \frac{27^{1/3}}{125^{1/3}}$

Since $27 = 3^3$ and $125 = 5^3$, the expression simplifies to:

$\frac{R_P}{R_Q} = \frac{3}{5}$

Therefore, the ratio of their radii $R_P : R_Q$ is $3 : 5$, which corresponds to Option B.

content: In a nuclear reaction 2 deuteron nuclei combine to form a helium nucleus. The energy released in $$\mathrm{MeV}$$ will be: (Given mass of deuteron $$=2.01355 \mathrm{~amu}$$. and mass of helium nucleus $$=4.0028 \mathrm{~amu}$$.
explanation:
To find the energy released in this nuclear reaction, we use the concept of mass defect and Einstein's mass-energy equivalence principle. The mass defect is the difference between the mass of the reactants and the mass of the products in a nuclear reaction. According to Einstein's relation, $$E=mc^2$$, where $$E$$ is the energy released, $$m$$ is the mass defect, and $$c$$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

Given data:

  • Mass of deuteron = $$2.01355\, \mathrm{amu}$$
  • Mass of helium nucleus = $$4.0028\, \mathrm{amu}$$

The reaction involves two deuteron nuclei combining into one helium nucleus. First, let's calculate the total mass of the reactants (2 deuterons):

$$\text{Total mass of reactants} = 2 \times \text{mass of deuteron} = 2 \times 2.01355\, \mathrm{amu}$$

$$\text{Total mass of reactants} = 4.0271\, \mathrm{amu}$$

Now, let's find the mass defect ($$\Delta m$$), which is the difference between the mass of the reactants and the mass of the product (helium nucleus):

$$\Delta m = \text{Total mass of reactants} - \text{mass of product}$$

$$\Delta m = 4.0271\, \mathrm{amu} - 4.0028\, \mathrm{amu}$$

$$\Delta m = 0.0243\, \mathrm{amu}$$

To convert the mass defect from atomic mass units (amu) to kilograms (kg), we use the conversion factor $$1\, \mathrm{amu} = 1.660539040 \times 10^{-27}\, \mathrm{kg}$$:

$$\Delta m \,(\mathrm{kg}) = 0.0243\, \mathrm{amu} \times 1.660539040 \times 10^{-27} \, \mathrm{kg/amu}$$

$$\Delta m \, (\mathrm{kg}) = 4.03311 \times 10^{-29}\, \mathrm{kg}$$

The energy released can now be calculated using the formula $$E=mc^2$$, where $$c = 2.99792458 \times 10^8 \, \mathrm{m/s}$$ (speed of light):

$$E = (4.03311 \times 10^{-29}\, \mathrm{kg}) \times (2.99792458 \times 10^8 \, \mathrm{m/s})^2$$

$$E = 3.62894 \times 10^{-12}\, \mathrm{J}$$

To convert energy from Joules to Mega-electronvolts ($$\mathrm{MeV}$$), we use $$1\, \mathrm{J} = 6.242 \times 10^{12}\, \mathrm{MeV}$$:

$$E = 3.62894 \times 10^{-12}\, \mathrm{J} \times 6.242 \times 10^{12}\, \mathrm{MeV/J}$$

$$E = 22.65\, \mathrm{MeV}$$

The energy released is approximately $$22.65\, \mathrm{MeV}$$, which is closest to Option C, $$22.62\, \mathrm{MeV}$$. Therefore, Option C is the correct answer.

content: A particle at rest decays in to two particles of mass $$m_1$$ and $$m_2$$ and move with velocities $$v_1$$ and $$v_2$$. The ratio of their de Broglie wave length $$\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}$$ is:
explanation:
The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by the formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p},$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant and $p$ is the momentum of the particle. The momentum $p$ can be expressed in terms of the mass $m$ and velocity $v$ of the particle as $p = m \cdot v$.

For the two particles in question, their de Broglie wavelengths are given by:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{h}{p_1} = \frac{h}{m_1 v_1},$$

$$\lambda_2 = \frac{h}{p_2} = \frac{h}{m_2 v_2}.$$

To find the ratio $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}$, we divide the equations:

$$\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \frac{\frac{h}{m_1 v_1}}{\frac{h}{m_2 v_2}} = \frac{m_2 v_2}{m_1 v_1}.$$

Given that the original particle is at rest before decaying into two particles, by the conservation of momentum, the total momentum before decay must equal the total momentum after decay. Before the decay, the momentum is 0 (since the particle is at rest), and after the decay, it must still sum to 0. Thus, we have:

$$m_1v_1 = m_2v_2.$$

Substituting $m_1v_1 = m_2v_2$ into the equation for the ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths, we get:

$$\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \frac{m_2 v_2}{m_1 v_1} = 1.$$

Therefore, the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}$ is 1 : 1. The correct option is:

Option B 1 : 1

content: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is $$-13.6 \mathrm{~eV}$$. If the electron jumps from the $$3^{\text {rd }}$$ excited state to the ground state then the energy of the radiation emitted will be:
explanation:
To determine the energy of the radiation emitted when an electron in hydrogen transitions from the $3^{\text{rd}}$ excited state to the ground state, first, we need to understand what is meant by "ground state" and "excited states" in the context of a hydrogen atom.

The energy levels of an electron in a hydrogen atom are given by the formula:

$$E_n = -\frac{13.6 \ eV}{n^2}$$

where $E_n$ is the energy of the n-th energy level (with the ground state being $n = 1$) and 13.6 eV is the energy of the electron in the ground state. The ground state (n=1) has an energy of $-13.6$ eV. The $3^{\text{rd}}$ excited state would correspond to $n = 4$, since the excited states start counting after the ground state $n=1$ (first excited state is $n=2$, second is $n=3$, and so forth).

Therefore, the energy of the $3^{\text{rd}}$ excited state ($n=4$) is:

$$E_4 = -\frac{13.6 \ eV}{4^2} = -\frac{13.6 \ eV}{16} = -0.85 \ eV$$

To find the energy of the radiation emitted when the electron transitions from the $n = 4$ level to the ground state ($n = 1$), we calculate the difference in energy between these two levels:

$$\Delta E = E_{final} - E_{initial} = E_1 - E_4 = (-13.6 \ eV) - (-0.85 \ eV) = -13.6 \ eV + 0.85 \ eV = -12.75 \ eV$$

The negative sign indicates that energy is released, so the magnitude of the energy is 12.75 eV. Thus, the energy of the radiation emitted is 12.75 eV.

This corresponds to Option B.

content: In the head-on collision of two alpha particles $$\alpha_1$$ and $$\alpha_2$$ with the gold nucleus, the closest approaches are 31.4 fermi and 94.2 fermi respectively. Then the ratio of the energy possessed by the alpha particles $$\alpha_2 / \alpha_1$$ is:
explanation:
When we consider a head-on collision of two alpha particles with a gold nucleus, the closest approaches directly relate to the kinetic energies of the alpha particles. This relationship can be understood from the perspective of electrostatic repulsion and conservation of energy. The kinetic energy of each alpha particle is transformed into potential energy at the point of closest approach, since at that point, their velocities become zero, assuming a non-relativistic scenario.

For an alpha particle approaching a gold nucleus, the potential energy at the closest approach is given by the Coulomb potential energy formula:

$$ PE = \frac{k \cdot q_1 \cdot q_2}{r} $$

where:

  • $k$ is Coulomb's constant,
  • $q_1$ and $q_2$ are the charges of the alpha particle and gold nucleus, respectively,
  • $r$ is the distance of the closest approach, which corresponds to the distance at which the entire kinetic energy of the alpha particle has been converted into electrostatic potential energy.

Given that the charge of the alpha particle and the nucleus remain constant and considering $k$ is a constant as well, the potential energy (and hence, the initial kinetic energy) varies inversely with the distance of closest approach.

Thus, the ratio of the kinetic energies ($KE$) of the alpha particles can be expressed in terms of their closest approaches, $r_2$ for $\alpha_2$ and $r_1$ for $\alpha_1$, as follows:

$$ \frac{KE_2}{KE_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} $$

Substituting the given values of the closest approaches, 31.4 fermi for $\alpha_1$ and 94.2 fermi for $\alpha_2$, into the equation:

$$ \frac{KE_2}{KE_1} = \frac{31.4}{94.2} = \frac{1}{3} $$

Therefore, the ratio of the energy possessed by the alpha particles $\alpha_2 / \alpha_1$ is $1: 3$, corresponding to Option A.

content: Which of the following statement is true when a gamma decay occurs from the nucleus of an atom?

options.content: Mass number is reduced by 4 and atomic number remains the same
Mass number remains the same and atomic number increases by 1
Mass number and atomic number are not changed
Mass number is reduced by 4 and atomic number is reduced by 2

explanation:
Gamma decay refers to the emission of gamma rays from a nucleus. Gamma rays are high-energy photons and do not carry any charge. They are typically emitted from the nucleus during radioactive decay when the nucleus transitions from a higher energy state to a lower energy state. Unlike alpha and beta decay, gamma decay does not involve the emission of particles such as protons or neutrons from the nucleus. Therefore, the emission of gamma rays does not change the composition of the nucleus in terms of its protons and neutrons.

Given this understanding:

  • Option A - Incorrect. This describes alpha decay, where the mass number is reduced by 4 and the atomic number is reduced by 2, not gamma decay.
  • Option B - Incorrect. This is more characteristic of beta decay, where a neutron is converted into a proton, increasing the atomic number by 1, but the mass number remains unchanged. Again, not gamma decay.
  • Option C - Correct. During gamma decay, neither the mass number nor the atomic number of the atom is changed. Gamma decay only involves a change in the energy state of the nucleus, with no alteration in the number of protons or neutrons.
  • Option D - Incorrect. This also describes alpha decay, not gamma decay.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C: Mass number and atomic number are not changed during gamma decay.

content: During $$\alpha$$-decay, atomic mass of parent nuclei is
explanation: During $$\alpha$$-decay, atomic mass of parent nuclei is decreased by 4 units while atomic number is decreased by 2 units.

content: Which of the following series spectrum of hydrogen atom lies in ultraviolet region?
explanation: Lyman series lies in the ultraviolet region whereas Paschen, Brackett and Pfund series lie in the infrared region.

content: The wavelength of the second line of Balmer series is 486.4 nm. What is the wavelength of the first line of Lyman series?
explanation:
Wavelength in Balmer series for hydrogen are given by

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\lambda} & =R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) \\ & =R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right), n=3,4,5, \ldots \end{aligned}$$

The second line in Balmer series corresponds to $$n=4$$

Hence, $$\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right)=\frac{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}$$

or, $$\lambda_2=\frac{16}{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}}$$

The wavelength of the first line $$(n=2)$$ in Lyman series is

$$\begin{array} & \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(1-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}}{4} \\ \text { or, } \quad \lambda_1=\frac{4}{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{4}{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}} \times \frac{3 R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}=\frac{1}{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1=\frac{\lambda_2}{4}=\frac{486.4}{4}=121.6 \mathrm{~nm} \end{array}$$

content: The Lyman series of a hydrogen atom belongs in which category
explanation: Lyman series of a hydrogen atom belongs to the ultraviolet region of electromagnetic spectrum.

content:
If an electron in hydrogen atom jumps from an orbit of level $$n=3$$ to an orbit at level $$n=2$$, emitted radiation has a frequency of

(R = Rydberg's constant and c = velocity of light)
explanation:
When an electron jumps from orbit $$n_i$$ to $$n_f$$, then the frequency of emitted photon is

$$\nu = cR\left( {{1 \over {n_f^2}} - {1 \over {n_i^2}}} \right)$$

Here, $${n_i} = 3,{n_f} = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow \nu = cR\left[ {{1 \over {{{(2)}^2}}} - {1 \over {{{(3)}^2}}}} \right] = {{5Rc} \over {36}}$$

content: Ba-122 has half-life of 2 min. Experiment has to be done using Ba-122 and it takes 10 min to set up the experiment. It initially 80 g at Ba-122 was taken, how much Ba was left when experiment was started?
explanation:
Given, n = 2 min to 10 min = 5 half-lives and $$N_0=80$$ g

We know, $${N \over {{N_0}}} = {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n}$$

$$ \Rightarrow N = {{{N_0}} \over {{2^n}}} = \left( {{{80} \over {{2^5}}}} \right) = {{80} \over {32}} = 2.5\,g$$

content: When the speed of light becomes $$\frac{2}{3}$$ of its present value, then the energy released in a given atomic explosion would
explanation:
Energy released in the nuclear process is

$$E = m{c^2} - m{c^2}{\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^2} = \left( {1 - {4 \over 9}} \right)m{c^2} = {5 \over 9}m{c^2}$$

Hence, energy released decrease by a factor of $$\frac{5}{9}$$.

content: An electron of an atom transits from $$n_1$$ to $$n_2$$. In which of the following maximum frequency of photon will be emitted?
explanation:
For emission of energy, electron of H-atom must fall from higher energy state to lower energy state. Hence, options (a) and (c) are not possible.

In option (b), $$n_1=2,n_2=1$$

$$\therefore$$ Energy of emitted photon is given as

$$E' = - 13.6{Z^2}\left( {{1 \over {n_1^2}} - {1 \over {n_2^2}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow h\nu ' = - 13.6{Z^2}\left( {{1 \over {{2^2}}} - {1 \over {{1^2}}}} \right)$$

$$\nu ' = {{13.6{Z^2}} \over h} \times {3 \over 4}$$ ...... (i)

In option (c), $${n_1} = 2,{n_2} = 6$$, hence energy of emitted photon is given as

$$E'' = - 13.6{Z^2}\left( {{1 \over {{6^2}}} - {1 \over {{2^2}}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow h\nu '' = - 13.6{Z^2} \times {8 \over {36}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \nu '' = {{13.6{Z^2}} \over h} \times {8 \over {36}}$$ ..... (ii)

Hence, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\nu ' > \nu ''$$

content: Two protons are kept at a separation of 40 $$\mathop A\limits^o $$. F$$_n$$ is the nuclear force and F$$_e$$ is the electrostatic force between them. Then,
explanation:
Nuclear force F$$_n$$ are attractive in nature. As in the given case, the two protons are kept at a separation of 40 $$\mathop A\limits^o $$ (short range), hence in this range, nuclear force F$$_n$$ is much greater than the electrostatic force F$$_e$$.

i.e. $$F_n> > F_e$$

content: Two radioactive materials X$$_1$$ and X$$_2$$ have decay constant 5$$\lambda$$ and $$\lambda$$, respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of X$$_1$$ to X$$_2$$ will be $$1/e$$ after a time
explanation:
Decays constants of radioactive materials $$X_1$$ and $$X_2$$ is given as $$\lambda_1=5\lambda$$ and $$\lambda_2=\lambda$$

If $$N_0$$ be the initial number of nuclei in both radioactive materials, then according to radioactive decay law,

$${N_{{X_1}}} = {N_0}{e^{ - {\lambda _1}t}}$$

and $${N_{{X_2}}} = {N_0}{e^{ - {\lambda _2}t}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{{N_{{X_1}}}} \over {{N_{{X_2}}}}} = {{{N_0}{e^{ - {\lambda _1}t}}} \over {{N_0}{e^{ - {\lambda _2}t}}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{e}=e^{\left(-\lambda_1+\lambda_2\right) t}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\text { Given, } \frac{N_{X_1}}{N_{X_2}}=\frac{1}{e}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^{-1}=e^{-\left(\lambda_1-\lambda_2\right) t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\lambda_1-\lambda_2\right) t=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{1}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2} \\ & =\frac{1}{5 \lambda-\lambda}=\frac{1}{4 \lambda} \\ & \end{aligned}$$
